coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots loaded polycarbonate thermosensitive hydrogel: An innovative strategy for promoting infected wound healing

Liu, Yongjia

Wu, Peng
Xu, Wei
Ma, Xian

Hu, Jianshe
Chen, Siwen
Yang, Liqun
coredata.dc:description: The healing process of bacterial infection wounds is a complex and chronic issue that poses a significant threat to human health. Thermosensitive antibacterial hydrogels, which transition from sol to gel at body temperature, have emerged as a promising option for wound dressing to accelerate healing and prevent infections. In this paper, we present a thermosensitive hydrogel (PTPH) made from (Polyethylene glycol monomethyl ether)-b-(polytrimethylene carbonate)-b-(polyethylene glycol monomethyl ether) (mPEG12-PTMCn-mPEG12, PTP) triblock copolymers, showing gel transition properties at around 34 °C. Additionally, (spermidine)-(ortho-phenylenediamine) carbon dots (SCDs) were enclosed within PTPH (PTPH-SCDs) to provide antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. In vitro experiments demonstrated that PTPH-SCDs have good skin adhesion, excellent biocompatibility, and exceptional antimicrobial efficacy. In vivo studies on a Sprague-Dawley rat model infected with Staphylococcus aureus showed that PTPH-SCDs dressings enhance the healing of infected wounds by inhibiting bacterial proliferation, promoting collagen deposition, and stimulating angiogenesis. This research presents a novel approach for developing multifunctional wound dressing by incorporating carbon dots into aliphatic polycarbonate based temperature-responsive hydrogels for the treatment of infected wounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermosensitive hydrogel
Polycarbonate
Carbon dots
Antibacterial
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Wound healing assessment in an infectious cutaneous wound model
Biocompatibility and bacteriostatic properties of PTPH-SCDs
Evaluation of the efficacy of PTPH-SCDs on wound healing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation of PTPH-SCDs hydrogel and schematic diagram of temperature-sensitive properties. (b) Procedure diagram of skin wound healing for staphylococcus aureus infection.
Characterization of SCDs. (a) FT-IR of SCDs.(b, c) XPS, (d) TEM, and (e) Zeta potential characterization of SCDs.

Characterization of PTPs and SCDs. (a) Synthetic route of PTP. (b) FT-IR of PTP. (c) 1H NMR spectrum of PTP and (d) molecular weight change before and after HDI coupling. (e) DSC and (f) TGA curves of PTP and PTP-SCDs. (g) SEM image of PTP-SCDs.

Studies of the gel transition temperature and rheological behavior of PTPH: (a) Changes in the storage modulus with temperature for PTPH and PTPH-SCDs (1 Hz). (b) Relationship between viscosity and shear rate in the gel state for PTPH and PTPH-SCDs. (c) Repeated dynamic strain step testing (γ = 1 % or 1000 %, 10 rad./s) of the PTPH-SCDs at 37 °C. (d) Injectable properties of PTPH. Investigation of the gelation behavior using the tube inversion method for (e) PTPH and (f) PTPH-SCDs. (g) Skin adhesiveness of PTPH-SCDs: finger extension and finger bending.

(a) Cell viability of L929 cells after co-culture with cell culture medium immersion solution of PTPH and PTPH-SCDs for 72 h. (b) Photos of hemocompatibility results of different groups and the percentage of hemolysis rate. (c) Image of the antibacterial activity of SCDs and PTPH-SCDs against S. aureus and E. coli. Corresponding statistical data of colonies in different groups of (d) S. aureus and (e) E. coli. Each value is presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Assessment of hydrogel-promoted in vivo wound healing. (a) Experimental procedure for wound healing with different groups. and representative images of wounds at different times. (b) Traces of the wound healing process at different periods. (c) Quantification of wound contraction, and (d) quantification of the relative wound area. Histologic assessment of wounds from different groups. (e) H&E, Masson trichrome, and CD31 stained sections of wound tissues with different treatments on day 12. (f) Wound diameter length of each group at day 12. (g) Quantification of collagen deposition in each group at day 12. (h) Positive area analysis of CD31. Each value is presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.
Reaction Ratio between PT and PTP and Molecular Weight.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a: Determined by 1H NMR. b: Determined by GPC.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots loaded polycarbonate thermosensitive hydrogel: An innovative strategy for promoting infected wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The healing process of bacterial infection wounds is a complex and chronic issue that poses a significant threat to human health. Thermosensitive antibacterial hydrogels, which transition from sol to gel at body temperature, have emerged as a promising option for wound dressing to accelerate healing and prevent infections. In this paper, we present a thermosensitive hydrogel (PTPH) made from (Polyethylene glycol monomethyl ether)-b-(polytrimethylene carbonate)-b-(polyethylene glycol monomethyl ether) (mPEG12-PTMCn-mPEG12, PTP) triblock copolymers, showing gel transition properties at around 34 °C. Additionally, (spermidine)-(ortho-phenylenediamine) carbon dots (SCDs) were enclosed within PTPH (PTPH-SCDs) to provide antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. In vitro experiments demonstrated that PTPH-SCDs have good skin adhesion, excellent biocompatibility, and exceptional antimicrobial efficacy. In vivo studies on a Sprague-Dawley rat model infected with Staphylococcus aureus showed that PTPH-SCDs dressings enhance the healing of infected wounds by inhibiting bacterial proliferation, promoting collagen deposition, and stimulating angiogenesis. This research presents a novel approach for developing multifunctional wound dressing by incorporating carbon dots into aliphatic polycarbonate based temperature-responsive hydrogels for the treatment of infected wounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin injuries are a common occurrence in daily life. When the dermal layer is damaged, the skin forms a wound, which impairs its ability to protect the body [1–3]. The healing process of a wound is complex and involves various stages, including inflammation, regeneration of granulation tissue, epithelial regeneration, and remodeling [4]. Bacterial infection is a major obstacle to wound healing [5]. Bacteria growth within wounds not only causes infection and ulceration but also promotes bacterial growth through the formation of biofilms, which prolongs the healing process [6]. Additionally, the formation of a scab over a wound poses challenges to spontaneous healing due to the loss of proteins and moisture from the wound, as well as the reduced ability of dermal cells to migrate [7,8].

Traditional wound dressings, such as gauze and bandages, lack the ability to promote wound healing or exhibit antibacterial properties. Moreover, when these dressings are replaced, they often cause further damage to the wound due to scab formation [9,10]. In contrast, hydrogels have gained significant attention in the medical field due to their three-dimensional network structure, excellent biocompatibility, and optimal moisture retention and gas permeability [11–15]. Hydrogels can create a moist microenvironment for the wound, preventing scab formation as well as facilitating the division and migration of epithelial cells [4,16]. Thermosensitive hydrogels have garnered particular attention within the realm of hydrogels. Among hydrogels, the thermosensitive variations have been notably emphasized for their ability to remain in a liquid state at room temperature, enabling them to conform to irregularly shaped wounds. Upon exposure to body temperature, these thermosensitive hydrogels transform semi-solid gels, adhering effectively to the wound site [17–19]. Examples of thermosensitive hydrogels include Poloxamer, poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG)-polyester, and acrylamide-based polymers [20–22]. Aliphatic polycarbonates exhibit excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability. Additionally, compared to polyesters, aliphatic polycarbonates do not produce acidic degradation byproducts[23]. However, the investigation of aliphatic polycarbonates as thermosensitive hydrogels has been relatively limited. In comparison to polyesters, the non-acidic degradation profile of aliphatic polycarbonates suggests their potential as promising wound dressing for skin applications [24].

In addition to providing a moist environment for wounds, preventing infection is a critical aspect of the wound healing process. Antibiotics have been widely used to inhibit bacterial diseases, but their overuse has led to the proliferation of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) [25]. Various noble metal nanoscale antibacterial agents, such as gold (Au), silver (Ag), and platinum (Pt), have been developed [26–29]. Additionally, metal oxide nanoparticles, including copper oxide (CuO), zinc oxide (ZnO), and titanium dioxide (TiO2), have demonstrated effective broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity [30–32]. However, the cytotoxicity and high cost associated with these nanoparticles limit their applications in both academic research and clinical therapy. Carbon dots (CDs), which are small-sized carbon nanoparticles (2–10 nm), are easy to prepare with controllable structures. CDs are typically highly dispersible and stable in water, and they are generally biocompatible [5,33]. The most common antimicrobial mechanism of CDs involves the photogeneration of singlet oxygen or hydroxyl radicals, which non-selectively kill bacteria via oxidative stress[34,35]. Additionally, cationic CDs, characterized by their small size and positive charge, can easily interact with negatively charged bacteria. This interaction results in the disruption of bacterial membranes through electrostatic interactions, thereby compromising the integrity of bacterial biofilms [36–40]. While hydrogels containing CDs for antibacterial purposes have been extensively studied in wound treatment, the combination of thermosensitive hydrogels based on aliphatic polycarbonates with cationic carbon dots as wound dressings remains unexplored in existing literature.

In this study, we synthesized a mPEG12-PTMCn-mPEG12 triblock polymer and developed a polytrimethylene carbonate thermosensitive hydrogel (PTPH). Furthermore, SCDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method. By combining these two components, we created the PTPH-SCDs composite, which was then applied to a Sprague-Dawley (SD) rat skin defect model infected with Staphylococcus aureus. The objective was to investigate the effectiveness and biocompatibility of PTPH-SCDs in healing infected wounds. We evaluated the efficiency of wound healing, collagen deposition, and angiogenesis in the treated areas. The preparation of PTPH-SCDs and the process of staphylococcus aureus infected wound healing are shown in Fig. 1 .

Trimethyl carbonate (TMC, purity ≥ 99.8 %) was procured from Jinan Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China. mPEG (Mn = 550, purity ≥ 99.5 %), hexamethylene diisocyanate（HDI） (purity ≥ 98 %), and stannous octoate (purity 99.8 %) were sourced from Sigma Aldrich. Toluene and ether (purity ≥ 98 %) were supplied by Shenyang Chemical Reagent Factory, China. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (purity ≥ 99.5 %), dichloromethane (purity ≥ 99.5 %), and disodium hydrogen phosphate (purity ≥ 99.5 %) were provided by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. O-Phenylenediamine (purity ≥ 98 %) and spermine (purity ≥ 99 %) were obtained from Aladdin. Toluene was pre-dried by azeotropic reflux with sodium wire before use. Standard purification methods were employed for all other solvents used in this study.

The synthesis of the mPEG12-PTMCn-mPEG12 block polymer (PTP) was carried out as depicted in Fig. 2 (a). Anhydrous mPEG12 (Mn = 550) was employed as the macromolecular initiator, with a toluene solution of stannous octanoate serving as the catalyst. These components were precisely loaded into a desiccated polymerization tube, along with trimethylene carbonate (TMC), following specific molar ratios (monomer: initiator = 19:1, monomer: catalyst = 500:1). To ensure the complete removal of both toluene and ambient air, the system was subjected to a nitrogen replacement procedure, conducted thrice. Upon achieving an internal vacuum below 15 mmHg, the polymerization tube was sealed. The system was then immersed in an oil bath and maintained at a reaction temperature of 130 °C for 24 h to obtain mPEG12-b-PTMCn (PT). Subsequently, HDI was introduced to initiate the coupling reaction (HDI: mPEG12 = 1:2), which was allowed to proceed for 6 h at 60 °C before the reaction was terminated. The resultant polymer was dissolved in a suitably measured quantity of dichloromethane, precipitated in ice-cold diethyl ether, followed by vacuum drying and refrigeration. This comprehensive synthetic protocol culminated in a final yield of approximately 75 %.

Dissolve the synthesized PTP at a concentration of 40 % (w/w) in the prepared phosphate PBS buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4). Stir the solution at 4 °C until PTP is completely dissolved, resulting in the formation of PTPH.

The synthesis method of SCDs was modified from the previous reference[41]. The process commenced with the dissolution of 500 mg of spermidine and 500 mg of o-phenylenediamine in 40 mL of deionized water. This solution was subsequently transferred to a high-pressure reactor and subjected to a reaction at 230 °C for 4 h. Following the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature under natural conditions. The resulting suspension was then centrifuged at a speed of 15,000 rpm for 10 min. The next step involved the filtration of the system using a 0.22 μm filter membrane, ensuring the removal of larger particulates. The filtered substance was dialyzed in deionized water using a dialysis bag with a 500 Da molecular weight cut-off for 4 h, with an intervallic replacement of the deionized water every 30 min. Finally, the synthesized SCDs were subjected to freeze-drying, purifying the sample, and then stored at a temperature of 4 °C for future applications.

The pre-synthesized SCDs were dispersed at a concentration of 1 mg/mL into a pre-configured phosphate PBS buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4). PTP, comprising 40 % of the mass ratio, was then added to the SCDs dispersion. The block polymers were subsequently allowed to fully dissolve at 4 °C, resulting in the formation of PTPH-SCDs.
The characterization studies of PTP, SCDs, and PTP-SCDs are provided in SI.

The sol–gel transition temperature of PTPH-SCDs was determined via the inversion method and rheological testing. Concurrently, the modulus of PTPH-SCDs both before and after the gel transition was quantified. Furthermore, the relationship between the viscosity of PTPH-SCDs and the shear rate was thoroughly examined as well as strain testing at 37 °C. The details are available in SI.

The plate counting method was employed for the assessment of the in vitro antibacterial efficacy of hydrogels [42]. Detailed instructions for the in vitro antimicrobial testing can be found in the SI.
Cytocompatibility was assessed using direct contact testing between hydrogels and L929 cells. Details can be found in the SI.
Hemolysis testing was performed by modifying a previously described protocol [43]. Details can be found in the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing assessment in an infectious cutaneous wound model
ce:section.ce:para: A full-thickness skin defect model of Staphylococcus aureus infection was chosen to comprehensively evaluate the performance of hydrogel dressings. The wound area was photographed on days 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 and calculated using Image J. All animal studies were approved by the Ethics Committee of the Liaoning Research Institute for Eugenic Birth and Fertility. The exact procedure is described in SI.

On day 12, tissue samples were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 48 h, embedded in paraffin, and sectioned into 7 μm slices. These sections were then processed and stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome. Subsequently, immunostaining was performed using a solution containing anti-CD31 antibodies. Images were captured using a slide scanner (Aperio CS2).

Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Significant differences were considered when *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001.

The structure of SCDs was elucidated through FT-IR analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a), the absorption bands at 3052 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 were attributed to the C-H stretching vibrations of the methylene groups in the spermidine moiety. The broad peak observed at 3350 cm−1 was assigned to the N-H stretching vibration of the amine groups. The absorption peak at 1627 cm−1 corresponded to the N-H bending vibration, while the peak at 1271 cm−1 was associated with the C-N stretching vibration, confirming the presence of the spermidine and amine functionalities. The characteristic absorption bands of the benzene ring were observed at 1590 cm−1, 1570 cm−1, 1493 cm−1, and 1454 cm−1, corresponding to the C = C stretching vibrations. Additionally, the peaks at 817 cm−1 and 745 cm−1 were attributed to the out-of-plane C-H bending vibrations of the benzene ring. The absorption band at 745 cm−1 was assigned to the C-H bending vibration of the o-disubstituted benzene, indicating the presence of the o-phenylenediamine moiety. The XPS analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2 (b, c), revealed the elemental composition of the synthesized SCDs. The spectra showed the presence of carbon (284 eV), nitrogen (399 eV), and oxygen (531 eV) in the SCDs. The nitrogen species were primarily in the form of pyridine-N and graphitic-N, as evidenced by the deconvoluted peaks. The ultrasmall size and high positive surface charge of the SCDs are key factors that contribute to their ability to inhibit bacterial growth. As shown in the TEM image in Fig. 2 (d), the SCDs exhibited a tendency to aggregate and possessed sizes less than 10 nm, which is consistent with the inherent characteristics of ultrasmall carbon dots. Furthermore, the zeta potential of the SCDs was measured to be 49.13 mV, as depicted in Fig. 2 (e). This high positive surface charge of the SCDs is expected to confer them with the potential to effectively suppress bacterial growth. The fluorescent properties of SCDs were presented in Fig. S2. Under 365 nm laser excitation, SCDs exhibited yellow fluorescence emission.

The preparation process of PTP was illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). mPEG12 served as a macroinitiator, and TMC underwent ring-opening polymerization catalyzed by Sn(Oct)2, and was subsequently coupled with HDI to yield PTP. The structure of PTP was confirmed through FT-IR and 1H NMR analysis. In Fig. 3 (b), absorption peaks at 1103 cm−1 were attributed to C-O-C stretching vibrations in mPEG12, with the peak at 2974 cm−1 corresponding to C-H stretching vibrations of CH2 in the polymer chain. The absorption peak at 1747 cm−1 was associated with the characteristic C = O stretching vibration of the polycarbonate chain segment, and 1407 cm−1 represented the C-N stretching vibration of the amide bond. These results indicated that TMC polymerization was initiated by the terminal hydroxyl group of mPEG12, and successful coupling with PT via HDI to form an amide bond was confirmed. Further structural analysis of the obtained triblock copolymer was conducted using 1H NMR spectroscopy. In Fig. 3 (c), the signal at 3.65 ppm corresponded to –CH2 of the mPEG12 segment, while signals at 2.05 ppm and 4.24 ppm represented –CH2 of the PTMC block. Additionally, peaks observed at 1.26 ppm and 3.15 ppm were attributed to the two types of CH2 in HDI. These results supported that the structure of PTP was following the intended molecular design. The molecular weights of the PT and PTP were determined by GPC. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. 3 (d), the number-average molecular weight (Mn) of the PT was 2957 g/mol, while the Mn of the PTP polymer increased to 5886 g/mol after the coupling reaction with HDI. This significant increase in molecular weight confirmed the successful coupling of the PT segments to form the PTP block copolymer. The thermal properties of PTP and PTP-SCDs polymers were analyzed using DSC and TGA. The glass transition temperature (Tg) of a polymer is crucial in determining its state, whether glassy or highly elastic, under varying conditions. As depicted in Fig. 3 (e), the Tg values for PTP and PTP-SCDs were determined to be –32.6 and –33.1 °C, respectively. The absence of a melting peak during heating indicated the amorphous nature of the PTP polymer. Moreover, with a Tg below the human body's physiological temperature (37 °C), PTP remained soft at body temperature, making it a promising material for hydrogels. The decomposition temperature of PTP played a significant role in the choice of disinfection methods. In Fig. 3 (f), it was clear that the decomposition temperatures of PTP and PTP-SCDs surpassed 200 °C, highlighting the excellent thermal stability of PTP. This high thermal decomposition temperature allowed for the application of conventional high-temperature sterilization techniques on PTP without compromising its structural integrity, potentially providing a more cost-effective solution. The microstructure of PTPH-SCDs was examined through SEM analysis, which displayed a porous structure (Fig. 3 (g)). However, the low molecular weight of the PTP polymer and its amorphous, non-crystalline structure with creep characteristics led to the inability to maintain most of the pores for a prolonged period, resulting in their eventual disappearance due to creep. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S2, the PTPH-SCDs exhibited uniformly distributed yellow fluorescence emission under 365 nm laser excitation at 37 °C, analogous to that of SCDs, indicating the homogeneous dispersion of SCDs within the PTPH matrix at 37 °C.

The sol–gel transition of PTPH and PTPH-SCDs was induced by the dehydration of mPEG12 segments and the enhanced hydrophobic interactions between PTMC segments as the temperature gradually increased. The thermo-responsive transition properties of the hydrogels were investigated through rheological measurements using a rheometer. Initially, the study involved measuring the modulus shifts across different hydrogel types at a constant frequency of 1 Hz over a range of temperatures. The results revealed that the storage modulus (G') of PTPH exhibited a significant increase between 33–34 °C, indicating a sol–gel transition of the system at this temperature (Fig. 4 (a)). The incorporation of SCDs into PTPH to form PTPH-SCDs did not significantly alter the G' or the transition temperature, maintaining the gelation capability below 37 °C. In addition, the gel transformation capabilities of PTPH-SCDs were assessed using the tube inversion method. As depicted in Fig. 4(e, f), both PTPH and PTPH-SCDs exhibited high fluidity at room temperature, demonstrating pronounced flow characteristics. Upon reaching 37 °C, both PTPH and PTPH-SCDs transitioned into a gel state, exhibiting exceptional thermosensitive properties. This demonstrated that PTPH and PTPH-SCDs possess the ability to undergo a sol–gel transition below human body temperature, indicating their potential for application in skin wound healing. Subsequently, the viscosity of the hydrogels under different shear rates was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the viscosity of PTPH and PTPH-SCDs reached a peak at approximately 1 rad./s. This behavior can be attributed to the presence of hydrophobic interactions within the hydrogels, which resist shear stress in the low shear rate range. When the shear rate exceeded 1 rad./s, the hydrophobic interactions were disrupted, leading to a rapid decrease in viscosity. This observation suggests that the hydrogels exhibit characteristics of viscous liquids under applied shear force. Additionally, Fig. 4(d) illustrates the formation of a “NEU” pattern through injection at 37 °C, confirming the excellent injectability of PTPH-SCDs. Consequently, PTPH-SCDs shows promise as a viable option for covering irregular wounds through injection. Wound sites often experience disruptions such as dressing breakage or detachment due to movement; therefore, the self-healing ability and adhesion to the skin of hydrogels, when employed as wound dressings, also require evaluation. Fig. 4(c) illustrated that after undergoing five repeated scanning cycles, the modulus of PTPH-SCDs was able to return to its original value when subjected to a 1 % strain. This observation highlights the remarkable self-healing ability of PTPH-SCDs. In the context of the amphiphilic polymer PEG12-PTMCn-PEG12, the transition from sol to gel phase was primarily triggered by the aggregation of hydrophobic segments at the temperature corresponding to the sol–gel transition [44]. Concurrently, these hydrophobic segments also functioned as reversible crosslinking points, enabling unrestricted movement and the creation of new aggregates following a 1000 % strain scan. This feature gives PTPH-SCDs a certain level of self-healing capability. [45]. Additionally, PTPH-SCDs exhibited robust adhesion to the skin, as evidenced by their application to finger joints and resistance to fracturing or detachment during repetitive flexion and extension, as depicted in Fig. 3(g). Based on the thermosensitive properties, injectability, favorable adhesion to the skin, and self-healing capabilities of PTPH-SCDs, they demonstrate significant potential as wound dressings. Based on the thermosensitive properties, injectability, favorable adhesion to the skin, and self-healing capabilities, PTPH-SCDs possess considerable potential as wound dressings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and bacteriostatic properties of PTPH-SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the performance evaluation of hydrogels, the biocompatibility of hydrogel dressings is also crucial. Fibroblasts play a crucial role in wound healing by producing the extracellular matrix and collagen, emphasizing the importance of hydrogel biocompatibility for fibroblast function. The study investigated the biocompatibility of PTPH, SCDs and PTPH-SCDs with L929 cells, as shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. 5 (a).The results after 72 h revealed a cell viability of 93.5 % for the PTPH group,91.6 % for the PTPH-SCDs group and 88.4 % for the SCDs both exceeding 85 %, indicating good biocompatibility of PTPH-SCDs. Additionally, Adequate blood compatibility is vital for biomaterial use in wound healing. The blood compatibility of PTPH-SCDs was assessed through in vitro hemolysis tests, showing minimal hemolysis with a rate of less than 5 %, confirming favorable blood compatibility (Fig. 5(b)). In the presence of wound infection, the antimicrobial efficacy of hydrogels is crucial. Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli strains were selected for in vitro assessment of the antimicrobial properties of SCDs and PTPH-SCDs. Results depicted in Fig. 5(c-e) demonstrated the complete elimination of both bacterial strains upon exposure to SCDs and PTPH-SCDs. The superior antimicrobial performance of SCDs can be attributed to their surface amino groups, positive charge, and smaller particle size, facilitating rapid binding with bacteria and disrupting their surface charge, leading to bacterial death. PTPH-SCDs displayed significant antimicrobial effects, potentially due to the release of SCDs by the hydrogel. With outstanding biocompatibility and remarkable antimicrobial properties, PTPH-SCDs hold promise as dressings for infected wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the efficacy of PTPH-SCDs on wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the role of PTPH-SCDs in promoting wound healing in vivo, a full-thickness skin defect model infected with Staphylococcus aureus was established in SD rats, as depicted in Fig. S4. The experimental treatment details are summarized in Fig. 6 (a). Following the creation of a 10 mm diameter skin defect, various group treatments were administered, including the PTPH group and the PTPH-SCDs group. After gelation at 37 °C, PTPH and PTPH-SCDs were uniformly injected into the wound site using a syringe, with untreated wounds serving as the Ctrl group. Tegaderm™ film was used to cover the wounds of all groups. Wound photographs were taken on day 3, 6, and 12, as shown in Fig. 6(a). To elucidate the wound healing process more clearly, traces of wound healing were drawn from the photographs (Fig. 6(b)), and the changes in wound area were tracked to analyze the efficiency of wound healing (Fig. 6(c,d)). Quantitative analysis of the wound area revealed no significant differences among the groups on day 3 and 6. However, by day 12 of wound healing, both the PTPH group (healing rate of 89.33 ± 3.39 %) and the PTPH-SCDs group (healing rate of 98.73 ± 0.25 %) demonstrated significantly better treatment effects on full-thickness skin defects compared to the Ctrl group (healing rate of 77.58 ± 3.80 %), with the PTPH-SCDs group almost achieving wound closure. This can be attributed to the moistening effect of PTPH on the wound surface, which accelerates the exchange of nutrients and inhibits wound scabbing, while the released SCDs can rapidly kill bacteria within the wound and suppress biofilm growth. Although the healing rate of the PTPH group was lower than that of the PTPH-SCDs group, it was still higher than that of the Ctrl group. This is attributed to the ability of PTPH to maintain wound moisture and physical isolation, which can accelerate nutrient exchange and prevent crusting, thereby promoting wound healing. These findings indicate that PTPH-SCDs possess advantageous properties in promoting the regeneration of infected wounds. Further investigation into the mechanisms of wound healing was conducted through histological and immunohistochemical analyses, as described in the following section.

Histological analysis of the regenerated skin 12 days post-healing was performed using H&E and Masson trichrome staining (Fig. 6(e)). The PTPH-SCDs group showed a significant decrease in neutrophils and lymphocytes near the wounds compared to the Ctrl group, resulting in a smaller epidermal wound diameter of 0.47 ± 0.82 mm. In contrast, the Ctrl group exhibited varying levels of inflammatory cell infiltration with a larger wound diameter of 4.1 ± 0.38 mm. The wound diameter in the PTPH group was 3.57 ± 0.52 mm, smaller than the Ctrl group but larger than the PTPH-SCDs group (Fig. 6(f)). This suggested that PTPH-SCDs have a remarkable ability to enhance the epithelialization of infected wounds due to their superior antibacterial properties. By eradicating bacteria at the wound site, PTPH-SCDs reduced inflammation and promoted epidermal remodeling. Additionally, Masson's trichrome staining revealed higher collagen deposition in the PTPH-SCDs group compared to other groups (Fig. 6(g)), at 19.07 ± 2.03 %, almost double that of the Ctrl group (8.25 ± 0.42 %). The accelerated epithelial formation and collagen deposition observed in the PTPH-SCDs group compared to the Ctrl group suggest that PTPH-SCDs have the potential to expedite wound healing by enhancing collagen deposition. In addition to collagen deposition, angiogenesis plays a crucial role in wound healing, as newly formed granulation tissue heavily depends on vascularization for nutrient transport [46]. CD31, a transmembrane protein expressed in early angiogenesis, serves as a marker for evaluating neovascularization. To delve deeper into the mechanisms of wound healing, we conducted an immunohistochemical analysis of CD31 (Fig. 6(h)). By day 12, the area positive for CD31 in the PTPH-SCDs group was lower than both the Ctrl and PTPH groups, indicating progression to a later stage of wound healing. Compared to the Ctrl group, the PTPH-SCDs group showed numerous larger-diameter mature vessels, suggesting the gradual maturation of granulation tissue during wound healing. Some capillaries collapse, while others transform into small arteries and veins to meet the demands of normal skin function [47,48]. Based on the results presented above, PTPH-SCDs may accelerate the healing of infected wounds by promoting angiogenesis.

In summary, we developed a novel mPEG12-PTMCn-PEG12 triblock copolymer hydrogel (PTPH) for use as a wound dressing. The PTPH exhibited a gelation transition temperature below human body temperature (∼34 °C), injectability, self-healing properties, and strong adhesion to skin. SCDs were effectively loaded into PTPH, resulting in PTPH-SCDs that demonstrated excellent biocompatibility and a remarkable capability to inhibit the proliferation of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. In a full-thickness Staphylococcus aureus infection model in SD rats, PTPH-SCDs inhibited bacterial growth, and promoted collagen deposition and angiogenesis, significantly accelerating the wound healing process. The application of PTPH-SCDs in promoting the healing of infected wounds has shown tremendous potential, making it suitable for biomedical applications.

Baopeng Guo: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology. Yongjia Liu: Data curation. Siyu Han: Data curation. Peng Wu: Data curation. Wei Xu: Data curation. Xian Ma: Software. Zhiyu Zhang: Software. Shigao Peng: Software. Jianshe Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Resources. Siwen Chen: Writing – original draft, Project administration. Liqun Yang: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (2022-YGJC-69), the Support Program for Excellent Young Scholars of China Medical University, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2305004), the College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Northeastern University (230262), and Shenyang Science and Technology Bureau (RC220479).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2024.113230.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
